description: Nasal diphthong with no clean English equivalent. Start toward 'ow' but let the sound resonate through the nose. Found in many plurals (pão → pães, but coração, não). Contrast it with plain oral vowels: 'não' (nasal diphthong) vs 'no' (plain [nu]).
Word-initial 'r' and double 'rr' are pronounced like an English 'h' (or a raspy back-of-throat sound) in most of Brazil. 'Rio' sounds like 'Hee-oo'.

Before an 'i' sound (and often final unstressed -e), 'd' and 't' palatalize: 'de' → 'jee', 'ti' → 'chee'. 'cidade' ends like 'jee'. This happens word-internally too, not just at edges: 'existe' → 'eh-ZEES-chee', 'departamento' → 'jee-par-ta-MEN-tu', 'noite' → 'NOY-chee'.

minimalPairs.note: 'part' [ˈpaʁtʃi] vs 'I left' [paʁˈtʃi] — both end in [tʃi]; only the stress differs

description: A single nasal vowel, not a diphthong: say 'uh' while letting air escape through the nose, as if a faint 'ng' were fused into the vowel — but never actually pronounce an 'n' or 'g'. The tilde is the nasality switch: 'lã' (wool) is nasal, 'lá' (there) is oral. Also the sound in 'maçã', 'irmã', 'amanhã'.

Nasal diphthong: a nasal 'o' gliding into a nasal 'y' — roughly 'oyng' without ever closing into a real 'ng'. Appears in 'põe' (puts) and, crucially, in the plural of most -ão words: limão → limões, coração → corações, opinião → opiniões.

minimalPairs.note: plural õe vs singular ão — the two big nasal diphthongs contrasted

description: Word-final -em/-ém is not 'em' as in English — it's a nasal diphthong: a nasal 'e' gliding into a nasal 'y', so 'bem' sounds close to 'baying' said through the nose, never with a hard m. Same in 'também', 'ninguém', 'trem', 'cem'. Word-internally before a consonant (tempo, sempre) the vowel is nasal but there's no glide.

minimalPairs.note: 'without' (nasal diphthong) vs 'I know' (oral diphthong) — same shape, nasality flipped; 'cem' (hundred) sounds identical to 'sem'

description: Unstressed final vowels reduce: final -o is pronounced [u] ('carro' = 'KA-hu', 'obrigado' = 'obri-GA-du') and final -e is pronounced [i] ('cidade' = 'si-DA-ji', 'nome' = 'NO-mi'). This is universal in Brazil and is what triggers the d/t palatalization in words like 'cidade' and 'noite'. Spelling keeps o and e; speech says u and i.

minimalPairs.note: 'bush / I kill' [ˈmatu] vs 'killed' [maˈtow] — final unstressed -o = [u]; stressed -ou keeps the diphthong

description: Stressed 'e' has two heights: open é [ɛ] as in English 'set' ('pé', 'café', 'ela') and closed ê [e] like the vowel in 'say' but without the glide ('você', 'mesa', 'ele'). Written accents disambiguate when present: acute (é) = open, circumflex (ê) = closed. Unaccented words you simply have to learn: 'ele' has closed e, 'ela' has open e.

minimalPairs.note: noun 'drought' (closed ê sound) vs verb 'dries' (open é sound) — same letters, different vowel height

description: Stressed 'o' also has two heights: open ó [ɔ] as in 'law' ('avó', 'só', 'nossa') and closed ô [o] as in 'go' without the glide ('avô', 'ovo', 'todo'). Acute = open, circumflex = closed. Gender and number can flip it: 'ovo' (egg, closed) vs 'ovos' (eggs, open first o) — a famous irregularity called metaphony.

A palatal L: like the 'lli' in 'million' said quickly, tongue body pressed against the hard palate. In 'filho', 'mulher', 'trabalhar', 'olho'. Many Brazilians relax it toward a plain 'y' glide in fast speech ('mulher' ≈ 'mu-YER'), so train your ear for both — but produce the [ʎ] version.

minimalPairs.note: 'old woman' (lh) vs 'vein' — in fast relaxed speech these can merge; careful speech keeps them apart

description: A palatal N, like the 'ny' in 'canyon': 'banho', 'sonho', 'amanhã', 'senhora'. In Brazilian Portuguese it's usually realized as a nasal glide [j̃] that also nasalizes the vowel before it — 'banho' sounds like 'BAH-yoo' said through the nose rather than a crisp 'BAN-yo'.

minimalPairs.note: 'vineyard / used to come' (nh) vs 'saw / route' (no nasal)

description: A single 's' between vowels is voiced [z]: 'casa' = 'KA-za', 'mesa' = 'ME-za', 'brasileiro' = 'bra-zi-LEY-ru'. To get an actual [s] sound between vowels, Portuguese spells it 'ss' ('assar') or 'ç' ('caça'). Getting this wrong is a classic tell: saying 'CA-sa' with [s] sounds like 'caça' (hunt).

Word-initial 'x' is always 'sh': 'xícara' (cup) = 'SHEE-ka-ra', 'xarope' = 'sha-RO-pi', 'Xuxa' = 'SHOO-sha'. ('ch' spells the same sound: 'chá', 'chuva'.) Elsewhere x is unpredictable — [ʃ] in 'caixa', [z] in 'exame', [ks] in 'táxi', [s] in 'próximo' — and must be learned per word.

minimalPairs.note: 'chin' (x = ʃ, 'sh') vs 'cheese' (j = ʒ, voiced) — the unvoiced/voiced pair

description: The cedilla makes c sound as [s] before a, o, u — where a plain c would be [k]: 'praça', 'açúcar', 'almoço', 'coração'. It is never a 'ch' or 'ts' sound, and it never appears before e or i (plain c is already [s] there: 'cedo', 'cidade').

minimalPairs.note: 'mace' and 'dough' — homophones: ç and ss spell the same [s]

description: Portuguese j — and g before e or i — is the 's' of English 'measure' [ʒ], never the English 'j' [dʒ]: 'janela' = 'zha-NE-la', 'gente' = 'ZHEN-chi', 'girafa' = 'zhi-RA-fa', 'hoje' = 'O-zhi'. Saying 'Rio de Janeiro' with an English j is one of the most audible gringo markers.

minimalPairs.note: 'delicacy / to get (slang)' [ʒ] vs 'to stain' [ʃ] — voiced vs unvoiced

description: At the end of a syllable, 'l' becomes a 'w' glide in Brazilian Portuguese: 'Brasil' = 'bra-ZEEW', 'legal' = 'le-GAW', 'sol' = 'sow', 'alto' = 'AW-tu'. There is no dark L as in English 'ball'. This merges some spellings completely: 'mal' (badly) and 'mau' (bad) are perfect homophones.

minimalPairs.note: perfect homophones in Brazil — both [maw]; only spelling and grammar distinguish them

description: Syllable-final r is the great regional divider. Rio: a strong raspy [χ/h] — 'porta' ≈ 'POH-hta', 'mar' ≈ 'mah'. São Paulo city: a light tap [ɾ]. Interior SP/Paraná ('caipira' accent): an English-like retroflex [ɹ] — 'porta' with an American r. Everywhere, infinitive-final r is usually just dropped in relaxed speech: 'falar' → 'falá', 'comer' → 'comê'.

minimalPairs.note: 'to speak' vs 'speaks' — with the final r dropped, only the stressed final vs penultimate syllable distinguishes them in fast speech
'sea' vs 'bad (fem.)' — near-merger in Rio speech where the coda r weakens

description: Between vowels the two r's contrast and change word meaning. Single 'r' is a quick tap [ɾ], exactly the tt/dd in American 'butter'/'ladder'. Double 'rr' is the strong h-like sound. English speakers' instinct to say a full American r for single 'r' is wrong on both counts — drill the tap until 'moro' and 'morro' are effortless to keep apart.

In relaxed Brazilian speech the diphthong 'ei' commonly smooths to plain [e], especially before j, x, and r sounds: 'beijo' → 'bejo', 'cadeira' → 'cadera', 'primeiro' → 'primero', 'peixe' → 'pexe'. Careful speech keeps the glide. You should recognize the reduced forms instantly — they're what you'll actually hear — and either pronunciation from you is fine.

minimalPairs.note: 'street market' vs 'beast' — reduced 'feira' can sound very close to 'fera'; context disambiguates

description: Default stress: words ending in a/e/o (plus -s, -m) stress the second-to-last syllable ('caSA', 'faLAM'); words ending in r, l, z, i, u, or a diphthong stress the last ('faLAR', 'aQUI', 'paPEL'). Any written accent overrides the default and marks the stressed syllable — and its shape tells vowel quality too: acute (á/é/ó) = open, circumflex (â/ê/ô) = closed. Misplaced stress changes words outright.

minimalPairs.note: 'wise woman' (SÁ-bia) vs 'song-thrush' (sa-bi-Á) — same letters, accent moves the stress
'knew' (sa-BI-a, default rule) vs 'thrush' — the famous minimal triple with 'sábia'
